steps.title: Read Camillo's qualification
steps.content: Camillo says he might be "some allay" to the Sicilian king's sorrows, then adds that he may merely presume this. The underlined portion presents a hoped-for benefit while admitting uncertainty.

steps.title: Reject stronger claims
steps.content: Choice A wrongly focuses on comforting Polixenes. Choice B overstates the portion as an attempt to justify leaving. Choice D claims concern for the Sicilian king is Camillo's primary motivation, but the text first gives other reasons. The underlined portion marks the benefit as speculative. The answer is C.
